title: Japan
dek: An eight-day autumn run down the classic route — Tokyo, a Mt Fuji day, the Shinkansen to Kyoto, and a Nara hop — timed for the turning leaves. Everything below is checked; tick items off as you go and your progress saves.
footer: Location photos via Wikimedia Commons — tap any credit for its source, author and license.
intro: Tokyo first, then the Shinkansen to Kyoto, with day trips to Hakone (Mt Fuji) and Nara. Built for an October–November visit.

sections.title: Money & currency
sections.body:
Japan uses the yen (¥), and the yen is very weak right now — great for your budget. Rough rates (approximate, check before you go), at about ¥160 to the dollar: ¥100 ≈ $0.63 and ¥1,000 ≈ $6.25.

Japan is still surprisingly cash-friendly — carry some, especially for small shrines, markets and older shops — but cards and IC-card tap-to-pay now work in most places. Tipping isn't done and can confuse staff.

sections.title: When you land
checklist: Clear immigration — your Visit Japan Web QR codes (done before the flight) speed up immigration and customs
Get an IC card: a physical Welcome Suica at the counter, or add Suica to Apple Wallet if you have an iPhone
From Narita: Keisei Skyliner (~41 min to Ueno) or the N'EX; from Haneda: Keikyu/monorail (~30–45 min)
Text TRAVEL to 4004 for Verizon TravelPass, or use a local eSIM/SIM
Install Google Maps (works well in Japan) plus a translation app

sections.title: Local essentials
sections.body: Emergencies: 110 (police), 119 (fire & ambulance). Tap water is safe. No tipping. Trains are punctual and quiet — keep calls and speakerphone off. Carry a small bag for your own rubbish; public bins are rare. Convenience stores (7-Eleven, Lawson, FamilyMart) are lifesavers for cash (7-Bank ATMs take foreign cards), food and umbrellas.

sections.title: Entry & documents
sections.body: ⚠️ The entry rules below assume a US passport. On another passport, check your own requirements — they differ.
checklist: No visa for tourism up to 90 days
JESTA (Japan's new travel authorisation) is not required yet — it isn't expected until ~2028, so it doesn't affect a 2026 trip
Register on Visit Japan Web before you fly for the fast-track immigration & customs QR codes
Passport valid for the length of your stay
Type A/B plugs, 100V — most US chargers work; bring a small power strip
Autumn is peak season — book hotels early, especially Kyoto

sections.title: Should you buy a JR Pass?
sections.body: For this Tokyo–Kyoto route, almost certainly not. Since the 2023 price hike a 7-day national JR Pass is ¥50,000, while a Tokyo–Kyoto round trip in individual Shinkansen tickets is only about ¥27,000 — so the pass loses you roughly ¥20,000 unless you're also running far afield (Hiroshima, the far north). The better plan here: buy point-to-point Shinkansen tickets (ticket machines, or the smartEX app) and tap your IC card for everything local. Note the overseas pass price rises again on Oct 1, 2026.

sections.title: Phone & data
sections.body: Verizon TravelPass is $12/line/day and works in Japan; a local eSIM is cheaper if you can use one. Unlike Korea, Google Maps works well in Japan for trains and walking. Handy apps: a translation app (Google Translate or DeepL), smartEX for Shinkansen tickets, and your airline's app. Pocket Wi-Fi rental is an option for a group sharing one device.

sections.title: Weather, foliage & comfort
sections.body: October is mild but the tail of typhoon season, so watch forecasts; November is cool, crisp and gorgeous (roughly 7–17°C in the cities). For autumn colour: the mountains turn first — Nikko, Hakone and the Alps in late Oct–early Nov — while Tokyo and Kyoto peak late Nov into early December, and warm years run later still, so check the Japan Meteorological Corporation forecast near your dates. Pack layers and a compact umbrella. For heavier feet and heel pain, use a firm, structured insole with a deep heel cup and a weight rating (Powerstep Pinnacle Maxx, Tread Labs Pace, Superfeet, or VALSOLE / Walkomfy) — temple days are long and stone-paved.

sections.title: Key transit routes
steps: Airport → Tokyo: from Narita, the Keisei Skyliner (~41 min to Ueno) or the JR N'EX; from Haneda, the Keikyu line or monorail (~30–45 min). Sort an IC card first.
Around Tokyo: the JR Yamanote loop plus the metro cover almost everything — just tap your IC card in and out.
Tokyo → Kyoto: the Tokaido Shinkansen, ~2 hr 15 min on a Nozomi (about ¥14,000). Buy point-to-point (machines or smartEX); reserve a seat in autumn.
Day trip to Hakone: the Odakyu line from Shinjuku; the Hakone Free Pass covers the scenic loop (mountain railway, ropeway, pirate ship, bus) and Fuji views on clear days.
Kyoto → Nara: ~45 min by JR or Kintetsu — an easy half-day.
Flying home: from Kyoto, the Haruka express reaches Kansai (KIX) in ~80 min; or Shinkansen back to Tokyo if you fly from Narita/Haneda.

items.title: Arrival in Tokyo
items.pace: Arrival day · airport → city ~45–60 min · easy evening
items.note: After a long flight, keep it light — a neighbourhood izakaya and an early night.
items.body: Clear immigration (Visit Japan Web ready), train into the city, check in, and find dinner nearby. Don't fight the jet lag.
items.fit: Comfortable, layered clothes for cool autumn evenings.
checklist: Visit Japan Web QR codes saved offline
IC card set up (Welcome Suica or Apple Wallet)
TravelPass / eSIM on

items.title: Old Tokyo — Asakusa & Ueno
items.pace: Full day · Senso-ji ~1.5 hr · Ueno ~2 hr · Akihabara evening · lots of walking
items.note: Temple grounds are stone-paved and long — pace it and sit when you can.
items.body: Start at Senso-ji in Asakusa and the Nakamise shopping street, wander Ueno's park and museums, then Akihabara after dark for the electric, anime-and-games sensory overload.
items.fit: Walking shoes; a light layer for the evening.
checklist: Coins for the temple
Try a conveyor-belt sushi lunch

items.title: Modern Tokyo — Shibuya & Harajuku
items.pace: ~6 hr out · Meiji Shrine ~1 hr · Harajuku ~2 hr · Shibuya evening
items.note: Crowd-heavy and on your feet — build in a café break.
items.body: Quiet morning at Meiji Shrine, the youth fashion of Harajuku's Takeshita Street, then the Shibuya Scramble and a skyline view from Shibuya Sky at dusk.
items.fit: Stylish-but-comfortable; you'll be photographed a lot here.
checklist: Book Shibuya Sky ahead
See the Hachiko statue

items.title: Day trip — Hakone & Mt Fuji
items.pace: Long day out · ~1.5 hr each way · scenic loop ~5–6 hr
items.note: Fuji is shy — clear mornings give the best odds, and autumn colour is good here now.
items.body: Take the Odakyu line to Hakone and ride the loop — mountain railway, ropeway over the sulphur vents at Owakudani, and the lake with Fuji behind on a clear day. Soak in an onsen before heading back.
items.fit: Warm layers for the ropeway; bring a small towel for the onsen.
checklist: Hakone Free Pass
Check the Fuji forecast
Onsen etiquette: wash first, no swimsuits

items.title: Shinkansen to Kyoto
items.pace: Travel + sights · bullet train ~2 hr 15 · afternoon in Higashiyama
items.note: Reserve your Shinkansen seat in autumn; trains fill up.
items.body: Glide to Kyoto by Shinkansen (Fuji on the right early on), drop bags, and spend the afternoon climbing to Kiyomizu-dera through the Higashiyama lanes — best in late-afternoon light.
items.fit: Comfortable shoes for hills and steps.
checklist: Seat reserved (smartEX / machine)
Hotel near a subway/JR line

items.title: Kyoto — Fushimi Inari & Arashiyama
items.pace: Full day · Fushimi Inari early ~2 hr · Arashiyama ~3 hr · Gion evening
items.note: Beat the crowds: Fushimi Inari at sunrise, Arashiyama bamboo before mid-morning.
items.body: Go early to the thousands of vermilion torii at Fushimi Inari, then west to the Arashiyama bamboo grove and Tenryu-ji's autumn garden. End in Gion at dusk, the lantern-lit geisha quarter.
items.fit: Layers; a lot of walking and some climbing at Fushimi Inari.
checklist: Sunrise start for the torii
Be respectful in Gion — no chasing geiko for photos

items.title: Day trip — Nara (or Kinkaku-ji)
items.pace: Half to full day · Nara ~45 min each way · ~4–5 hr there
items.note: The deer are wild — buy crackers, but they'll headbutt for them.
items.body: Hop to Nara for the bowing deer in the park and the colossal bronze Buddha at Todai-ji. Back in Kyoto, fit in the Golden Pavilion (Kinkaku-ji) if you have the legs for it.
items.fit: Comfortable shoes; the park is large.
checklist: Deer crackers (shika senbei)
Todai-ji's Great Buddha Hall

items.title: Fly home
items.pace: Departure · Kyoto → KIX ~80 min (Haruka) · be there ~3 hr before
items.note: Allow a generous buffer — and use the luggage-forwarding (takkyubin) if your bags are heavy.
items.body: The Haruka express to Kansai Airport (or Shinkansen back to Tokyo if you fly from there). Last konbini snacks for the road.
checklist: Haruka / Shinkansen booked
Tax-free paperwork if you shopped
Passport + boarding pass

items.name: Senso-ji (Asakusa)
items.body: Tokyo's oldest temple, approached through the giant red Kaminarimon lantern and the Nakamise souvenir street. Lively day and night; come early to photograph it without the crowds.

items.name: Shibuya Crossing
items.body: The world's busiest pedestrian scramble — a wave of people on every light change. Watch from the Starbucks window or the Shibuya Sky deck above for the full effect.

items.name: Fushimi Inari Taisha
items.body: Thousands of vermilion torii gates winding up the mountain behind the shrine. The lower paths mob by mid-morning, so start at dawn and climb a little for near-empty tunnels.

img.file: Summit of Mount Inariyama @ Fushimi Inari Shrine @ Kyoto (13406530194).jpg

items.name: Arashiyama Bamboo Grove
items.body: A short, ethereal walk through towering bamboo on Kyoto's western edge, next to Tenryu-ji temple and its autumn garden. Go before mid-morning for the quiet and the light.

img.file: 2021 Sagano Bamboo forest in Arashiyama, Kyoto, Japan.jpg

items.name: Kinkaku-ji
items.body: A gold-leaf Zen pavilion mirrored in its pond — small but unforgettable, and stunning framed by autumn colour. A quick, flat visit you can pair with a Kyoto afternoon.

items.name: Kiyomizu-dera & Higashiyama
items.body: A wooden temple on stilts above Kyoto, reached through preserved lanes of shops and tea houses (Sannenzaka, Ninenzaka). Magic in late-afternoon light and autumn leaves.

sections.title: Visiting tips
sections.body: Japan's blockbuster sights reward early starts — Fushimi Inari, Arashiyama and Kiyomizu are transformed before 9am and exhausting after. Treat Kyoto temples as a morning game and save the afternoon for gardens and food. And don't over-stack the days: the country's joy is often the small stuff — a perfect konbini egg sandwich, a quiet shrine, a department-store food hall (depachika) at closing time.

sections.title: Akihabara & beyond
sections.body: Akihabara is the anime-and-electronics heart of Tokyo — multi-floor arcades, retro-game shops, figure stores, and themed cafés. Nakano Broadway is the collectors' maze for vintage goods, Pokemon Centers and the giant flagship stores dot the city, and in Kyoto/Osaka Den Den Town (Nipponbashi) plays Akihabara's role. Arcades (Taito, Round1) are everywhere for crane games and rhythm titles.

sections.title: What to eat
sections.body: Eat widely and cheaply: ramen, sushi (conveyor-belt or counter), tempura, okonomiyaki, tonkatsu, and standing soba. The depachika food halls in the basements of department stores are a feast at any budget, and convenience stores punch absurdly above their weight (the egg sandwich is famous for a reason). In Osaka-adjacent Kyoto, hunt down takoyaki and okonomiyaki.

sections.title: Shopping & tax-free
sections.body: Foreign visitors get tax-free shopping (the 10% consumption tax) at participating shops on purchases over ¥5,000 — bring your passport. Good hunting: Don Quijote (everything, late hours), Uniqlo / GU, drugstores for skincare, kitchenware in Kappabashi (Tokyo) and knives, and depachika sweets to bring home. Many shops do instant in-store tax-free; some refund at the airport.

sections.title: Souvenirs — Japan
items: KitKats in regional flavours (matcha, sake, Tokyo banana)
A folding fan, furoshiki cloth, or tenugui towel
Matcha and a good Japanese knife (Kappabashi)
Anime / game figures or goods from Akihabara

sections.title: Collectibles & chain merch
sections.body: For collectors: a Starbucks 'Been There' Tokyo / Kyoto mug, the Pokemon Center and Nintendo flagship stores for exclusives, and the Studio Ghibli-adjacent goods at Donguri Republic shops. Tokyo also has a Hard Rock Cafe (and Universal/Disney merch if those parks are on your list).

verified: ⚠ Draft — not yet fact-checked to the current standard; verify before relying on it
